title: Mugged

jokes.text: The entire opening phone call where Jemaine and Bret relay mundane information to their mom via each other rather than just talking directly to her
jokes.setup: Two grown men share a phone, one talking while the other whispers instructions on what to say, treating a simple phone call as an elaborate relay system
jokes.punchline: The content of the relay is completely banal — New Zealand mom is worried about safety, amazed there are more than four TV stations — making the elaborate system absurdly disproportionate to the stakes
jokes.explanation: A strong opener that establishes character perfectly — these two men are so codependent and socially awkward they can't even manage a phone call independently. The joke builds as we realize the mom's concerns are hilariously provincial (gun safety, TV stations). Character-integrated because this relayed communication style is distinctly Bret-and-Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Tell her it is a bit dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Not all Americans have guns.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Tell her Dave's got a paintball gun.

jokes.text: Jemaine whispering 'Tell her it is a bit dangerous' while Bret is in mid-sentence reassuring his mom it's not dangerous
jokes.setup: Bret is telling Mom 'It's not that dangerous here, not all Americans have guns'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine simultaneously contradicts this by whispering the opposite instruction, suggesting he actually is worried but won't say it himself
jokes.explanation: Efficient joke embedded inside the larger phone call bit. The conflict between what Bret says aloud and what Jemaine wants him to say reveals Jemaine's neurotic cowardice — he wants mom to know it's dangerous but won't own the statement himself.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Tell her about all the TV stations here, Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Jemaine wants me to tell you there's a lot of TV stations.
How many?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I don't know. There's a lot. Yeah yeah, no. More than four.

jokes.text: Jemaine whispering 'Tell her Dave's got a paintball gun' as evidence of American danger
jokes.setup: Jemaine is trying to convey that America is dangerous by citing gun ownership
jokes.punchline: The best example of American gun danger he can summon is a paintball gun belonging to their friend Dave
jokes.explanation: Beautiful economy — the punchline is the specific, deflating detail. Jemaine builds to 'gun ownership' as proof of danger, and his best example is Dave's paintball gun. The follow-up clarification 'It's not a real gun though, just shoots paint' makes it land harder. Classic FOTC deflation structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Murray, I was wondering if you could call me by my rapping name?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Your rapping name?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Rhymenoceros.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Rhinoceros?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: The Rhymenoceros.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'That's Bruce Willis, though. He's acting. It's not real.'
jokes.setup: Mom is presumably citing Bruce Willis movies as evidence that America is dangerous
jokes.punchline: Jemaine patiently explaining to his mother that Bruce Willis films are fictional — the clarification is so basic it implies the mom genuinely needed it
jokes.explanation: Quick, clean joke. The implied absurdity is that the mom's frame of reference for New York danger is Bruce Willis films, and Jemaine has to gently correct her. Competent character-world building for the provincial New Zealand context.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Nah, well, that's not gonna fit in here. I've got 'Bret.' Can't you just have Bret as your rapping name?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No. It's not a rapping name.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's a good rapping name. 'Hello, everybody, I am Bret.'

jokes.text: 'More than four.' — Bret describing the number of TV stations in New York to his amazed mother
jokes.setup: Jemaine insists Bret tell Mom about all the TV stations, implying this is amazing news
jokes.punchline: Bret's delivery of 'More than four' — the threshold for amazement is four channels, suggesting New Zealand has four or fewer
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical. The number 'four' as the baseline for amazement does a lot of world-building work in two words. The casual deadpan delivery of 'More than four. Yeah, yeah, no. More than four.' is perfectly Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Look, if you're gonna have some sort of rap name, just be careful you don't do a drive-by.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I won't.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, you'd better not.

jokes.text: After an elaborate phone call full of secondhand relaying — 'Who was that?' 'It's Mom. She's good.'
jokes.setup: We've just watched a full minute of anxious, complicated phone relay involving guns, TV stations, and Bruce Willis
jokes.punchline: The entire call is dismissed with a single flat sentence: 'It's Mom. She's good.'
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the opening scene. The comedic principle is maximum deflation — all that effort and anxiety compressed into 'She's good.' The scene cut [00:56] is itself the punchline. Structurally excellent: the setup is the entire preceding minute, the payoff is four words.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What about you, Jemaine? Have you got a rapping name?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, 'Hiphopopotamus,' but you can just call me Jemaine if you want.

jokes.text: Bret asking Murray to call him by his rapping name 'Rhymenoceros'
jokes.setup: Formal band meeting. Bret is a soft-spoken, mild-mannered New Zealander
jokes.punchline: He has given himself the rap name 'Rhymenoceros' — a portmanteau of 'rhyme' and 'rhinoceros' — and wants it used in official band meeting minutes
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. The contrast between the ultra-formal setting (band meeting minutes) and the aspiration to hip-hop identity is perfectly FOTC. 'Rhymenoceros' as a name is funny on its own — it's earnest, slightly wrong, and oddly logical. Sets up the payoff when Jemaine also has a rap name.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I mean, you don't hear of professional musicians with long hair.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, Led Zeppelin?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I mean a man.

jokes.text: Murray's counter-proposal: 'I've got Bret. Can't you just have Bret as your rapping name?' followed by performing it — 'Hello, everybody, I am Bret.'
jokes.setup: Bret wants 'Rhymenoceros'; Murray is the hapless manager trying to simplify
jokes.punchline: Murray literally demonstrates 'Bret' as a rap name by performing it — deadpan, no rhythm, entirely missing the point
jokes.explanation: Murray's obliviousness is perfectly calibrated here. He doesn't understand rap culture at all but is extremely confident. 'Hello, everybody, I am Bret' as a rap demonstration is brilliantly flat. Murray then adding 'Rhymes with wet' shows he's trying to be helpful while being completely wrong about what makes a rap name.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Most bands play at night.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's too dangerous out there at night.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We go around walking around at night all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, you know, anything could happen. You could get run over, pickpocketed, fall down a manhole, bump into people, murdered... imagine that... or even just ridiculed.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Rhymes with wet and...' — trailing off as if 'wet' is a sufficient endorsement for a rap name
jokes.setup: Murray is arguing 'Bret' is a good rap name
jokes.punchline: His sole supporting evidence is that it rhymes with 'wet' — and he can't think of anything else
jokes.explanation: The ellipsis is the joke — Murray ran out of reasons. Brief but effective. Layered into the larger Murray-incompetence bit.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We've never been ridiculed.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You haven't?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, that's a surprise. I get ridiculed all the time.

jokes.text: Murray warning Bret: 'If you're gonna have some sort of rap name, just be careful you don't do a drive-by.'
jokes.setup: Bret has a rap name. Murray is his manager offering practical advice
jokes.punchline: Murray's primary concern about rap identity is literal drive-by shootings — as if having a rap name automatically leads to gang violence, and this is a legitimate advisory note
jokes.explanation: Superb Murray moment. His casual conflation of 'having a rap name' with 'committing gang violence' is absurdly logical from his perspective as an out-of-touch New Zealander trying to manage his clients' safety. 'You know what I mean by that?' as a follow-up suggests he's slightly uncertain if they understand drive-bys, which is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh hey, ginger-balls.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You know?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That was Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: He called you that.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Hiphopopotamus — but you can just call me Jemaine if you want.'
jokes.setup: Murray asks if Jemaine also has a rapping name, presumably expecting Jemaine to be more sensible than Bret
jokes.punchline: Jemaine has an even more absurd name — 'Hiphopopotamus' — delivered with complete casualness and an immediate offer to waive it
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and among the best character-introduction moments in the series. 'Hiphopopotamus' is funny on three levels: (1) it's a great portmanteau, (2) Jemaine is clearly not a hippopotamus, (3) his casual 'but you can just call me Jemaine' completely deflates his own rap persona. The name also seeds the upcoming song. Economy is near-perfect — maximum information in minimum words. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor example in cleverness.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I thought that was your nickname?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No. I get, 'Oh, what are you on your way to... a dick meeting?'

jokes.text: Murray: 'And Murray... present.' — calling his own name in the roll call and answering it himself
jokes.setup: Murray is running the formal band meeting, calling attendance
jokes.punchline: He calls his own name and solemnly answers 'present' — maintaining procedural formality even for himself, as if he might not know if he's there
jokes.explanation: Small but perfectly formed. Murray's bureaucratic self-importance is captured in one beat. He is so committed to process that he must formally acknowledge his own attendance. Classic Murray character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You should wear these T-shirts with 'New York' on them. All right, just so you fit in. Here's a hat. Put this on, all right? 'Hey, I'm walkin' here!'

jokes.text: Murray: 'Item one, haircut. Bret.' / 'Well, get it cut. I mean, you don't hear of professional musicians with long hair.' / 'Well, Led Zeppelin?' / 'I mean a man.'
jokes.setup: Murray criticizes Bret's long hair as unprofessional for a musician
jokes.punchline: Bret correctly cites Led Zeppelin as a counterexample; Murray dismisses this with 'I mean a man' — a non-sequitur that implies Murray doesn't consider Led Zeppelin members to be men, delivered with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure. Murray makes a wrong claim → Bret devastates it with a perfect counterexample → Murray issues an illogical non-sequitur escape that is funnier than any real comeback could be. 'I mean a man' is quotable and reveals Murray's absurd worldview. Three-beat exchange, tight economy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: And these are the safety reflective belts. All right, they light up.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm not gonna wear that.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, you should.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, I'm not wearing it.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Yours is pretty long, Murray.'
jokes.setup: Murray has just insisted professional musicians don't have long hair and Bret needs a trim
jokes.punchline: Bret points out Murray's own hair is long, exposing his hypocrisy
jokes.explanation: Functional reversal joke. Simple but earns a laugh because Murray is oblivious to it. The setup was Murray's pomposity; the deflation is Bret's mild observation. Nothing novel, but good timing.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Would you rather get murdered?

jokes.text: Bret: 'What do you mean, another gig?' / Murray: 'Remember the other one?' / Bret: 'Vaguely.'
jokes.setup: Murray triumphantly announces he's going to get them 'another gig,' implying past success
jokes.punchline: Bret can only vaguely remember the previous gig, suggesting it was so minor and distant it barely registered as a real event
jokes.explanation: Tight three-beat exchange establishing the band's failure. 'Vaguely' as the answer to 'Remember the other one?' is perfectly calibrated — it implies the gig was so forgettable it barely exists in memory. Classic FOTC loser-band pathos played for dry laughs.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: You got a cigarette?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sorry, we don't smoke.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: Oh, that's too bad. Because my man Mickey here wants a cigarette, so now you're gonna have to give him some money.

jokes.text: Murray's list of nighttime dangers: 'get run over, pickpocketed, fall down a manhole, bump into people, murdered... imagine that... or even just ridiculed.'
jokes.setup: Murray is arguing against the band playing gigs at night for safety reasons
jokes.punchline: He lists dangers in escalating order — mundane (run over) → violent (murdered) → back down to mildly embarrassing (ridiculed) — treating 'ridiculed' as roughly equivalent to murder
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed list joke. The escalation to 'murdered... imagine that...' followed by the deflation to 'or even just ridiculed' is the key move — Murray puts social embarrassment in the same tier as death. The 'imagine that' pause before 'murdered' suggests he's genuinely visualizing it. Strong character integration: only Murray would rank these dangers this way. Approaches the 8.0 anchor.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: Listen, you don't want to mess with him, because he's crazy, man, all right? He's a psycho-killer. Qu'est-ce que c'est?

jokes.text: Murray: 'Well, that's a surprise. I get ridiculed all the time. Oh hey, ginger-balls. You know? That was Bret. He called you that.'
jokes.setup: Murray has listed ridicule as a major danger; Bret and Jemaine say they've never been ridiculed
jokes.punchline: Murray reveals he gets ridiculed constantly, then gives an example — only to reveal it was Bret who said it, not a stranger
jokes.explanation: Double pivot: we expect Murray to give an example from a stranger, but it's from Bret — his own client. Then Bret doesn't deny it. The reveal deepens Murray's pathos while simultaneously making Bret funnier. 'Ginger-balls' as the specific insult is appropriately odd.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, I've got $15.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: Okay, hand it over.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, but it's in the bank. We'd have to go to the bank to get it out.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: No, we don't have time to go to the bank, dipshit.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We might just have time.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I get, Oh, what are you on your way to... a dick meeting?'
jokes.setup: Murray is compiling his ridicule examples after 'ginger-balls'
jokes.punchline: The second insult is weirder and more specific than the first — 'a dick meeting' — implying a regular institutional gathering of dicks, which is more surreal than plain insults
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'dick meeting' is what makes it funny — it suggests Murray is being ridiculed by people with a surreal imagination, or that these are real insults Murray has memorized. Continues the escalation of Murray's ridicule sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: Why... why does the phone have a camera glued to it?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's a camera phone. Jemaine wanted one for his birthday.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Where'd you get it?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I made it. It's homemade.

jokes.text: Murray distributing 'I ♥ NY' tourist T-shirts, hats, and safety reflective belts to help them 'fit in'
jokes.setup: Murray is providing safety equipment to protect the band at night
jokes.punchline: His vision of 'fitting in' in New York is wearing full tourist gear — which would mark them as tourists, the most conspicuous and robbery-prone demographic — plus hi-vis safety vests
jokes.explanation: Strong visual joke with a logical inversion: the safety measures (tourist uniform + reflective belt) would actually make them more of a target. Murray distributing these items with genuine confidence is the essence of his character — enthusiastic, wrong, authoritative.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's a piece of shit.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, how come Jemaine likes it so much?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You can have it.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: No no, he doesn't want it.

jokes.text: Murray demonstrating New York attitude: 'Hey, I'm walkin' here!' — 'That's what they say when... people get in the way'
jokes.setup: Murray is teaching the boys to blend in by acting like New Yorkers
jokes.punchline: His masterclass in New York street vernacular is limited to one extremely generic movie-cliché phrase, delivered as genuine cultural insight
jokes.explanation: Murray's entire knowledge of New York culture appears to come from a single movie cliché. Functional joke, well-placed after the tourist gear bit. The explanation '...that's what they say when people get in the way' is perfectly unnecessary and perfectly Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You'd better be careful, because Bret knows karate.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got a book on karate. I haven't actually read it yet, but I've got...

jokes.text: Bret refusing the reflective safety belt: 'I'm not gonna wear that.' / Murray: 'Would you rather get murdered?'
jokes.setup: Bret resists wearing the humiliating hi-vis belt
jokes.punchline: Murray frames it as a binary choice: safety belt or murder — no middle ground exists in Murray's risk model
jokes.explanation: Tight. Murray's paranoid binary logic — reflective vest OR death — is exactly the kind of disproportionate risk assessment that defines his character. Callbacks to the 'murdered... imagine that' danger list earlier.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: Shut up, you English faggots, and give me the bike!

jokes.text: The mugging begins in medias res — the muggers are mid-story about someone getting stuck in a hole, then demand cigarettes, and pivot to robbery almost as an afterthought
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine are accosted by two men; we expect a classic threatening mugging scene
jokes.punchline: The muggers are mid-conversation about someone named Justin finding someone in a hole — they're casual, almost friendly, and the robbery emerges from 'do you have a cigarette?' rather than menace
jokes.explanation: Subverts mugging genre conventions immediately. By starting in the middle of a mundane conversation and having the robbery begin with a cigarette request, the show deflates the expected menace. Sets up the whole mugging sequence's tone.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You'd better watch out. You don't really know who you're dealing with.

jokes.text: John: 'You don't want to mess with him, because he's crazy, man, all right? He's a psycho-killer. Qu'est-ce que c'est? Give me some money.'
jokes.setup: John is trying to threaten Bret and Jemaine into handing over money
jokes.punchline: He vouches for Mickey's danger level, then spontaneously quotes Talking Heads lyrics ('Qu'est-ce que c'est' from 'Psycho Killer') as if it's a natural part of the threat, then immediately returns to the mundane demand
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent joke. John threatens Mickey is a 'psycho killer,' then reflexively quotes the Talking Heads song without acknowledging the reference — as if 'Qu'est-ce que c'est' is just something people say when they call someone a psycho killer. The non sequitur French phrase followed by 'Give me some money' is perfectly absurd. High originality, strong structure.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm the mother-flippin' Rhymenoceros / My beats are phat and the birds are on my back and I'm horny

jokes.text: Bret: 'Well, I've got $15.' / John: 'Okay, hand it over.' / Bret: 'No, but it's in the bank. We'd have to go to the bank to get it out.'
jokes.setup: John demands money; Bret establishes he has $15
jokes.punchline: Bret's $15 is entirely inaccessible — it's in the bank — and he states this with complete sincerity, as if this is a logistical problem they could solve together
jokes.explanation: Classic FOTC clueless sincerity. Bret announces his bank balance like it's relevant, then offers to go to the bank — treating the mugging as a cooperative financial transaction. John's exasperated 'We don't have time to go to the bank, dipshit' and Bret's 'We might just have time' escalate perfectly. Bret's obliviousness is total.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I was raised by a rapper and writer... they dated / And subsequently procreated

jokes.text: John: 'We don't have time to go to the bank, dipshit.' / Bret: 'We might just have time.'
jokes.setup: John refuses Bret's offer to go to the bank
jokes.punchline: Bret gently suggests they might actually have time — maintaining the cooperative framing, doing mental time-management on behalf of their mugger
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Bret's helpfulness is the joke. He's not arguing; he's genuinely trying to help the muggers get what they want by optimizing the bank trip. 'We might just have time' is the perfect oblivious response to being called a dipshit.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Flows, they glow like phosphorus / Poppin' off the top of the esophagus

jokes.text: John examining the homemade camera phone: 'Why... why does the phone have a camera glued to it?' / Bret: 'It's a camera phone. Jemaine wanted one for his birthday.' / John: '...it's a piece of shit.' / Bret: 'Well, how come Jemaine likes it so much?'
jokes.setup: Muggers go through Bret and Jemaine's pockets and find a phone with a camera physically glued to it
jokes.punchline: Bret's explanation — that it's a homemade 'camera phone' he made Jemaine for his birthday — transforms the mugging into a product review, and John's 'piece of shit' verdict becomes a moment of genuine evaluation
jokes.explanation: Among the best jokes in the episode. The mugging has completely derailed into a conversation about the quality of Bret's craft project. John — a mugger — is now the quality control authority on Bret's birthday gift technology. 'Well, how come Jemaine likes it so much?' is perfect: Bret is defending his work to his mugger. Originality is high; character integration is excellent (only Bret would have made a homemade camera phone as a gift). The detail that Jemaine wanted one for his birthday makes it tender and sad.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm not a large water-dwelling mammal / Where did you get that preposterous hypothesis? / Did Steve tell you that, perchance?

jokes.text: John deciding to keep the homemade camera phone: 'Yeah, I'll keep it. It's kind of interesting.'
jokes.setup: John has called the camera phone a 'piece of shit'
jokes.punchline: He keeps it anyway — his mugger-curiosity overrides his earlier dismissal, making him the second person to be charmed by Bret's terrible gift
jokes.explanation: Good button on the camera phone exchange. The mugger is now genuinely interested in the homemade tech despite his own negative review. Humanizes John in a funny way that pays off later.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: My rhymes and records, they don't get played / Because my records and rhymes, they don't get made / And if you rap like me, you don't get paid / And if you roll like me, you don't get laid

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Because Bret knows karate.' / Bret: 'Yeah, I got a book on karate. I haven't actually read it yet, but I've got...'
jokes.setup: Jemaine claims Bret knows karate as a threat
jokes.punchline: Bret confirms he has a karate book that he hasn't read — owning a book being the entirety of his martial arts qualification
jokes.explanation: Perfect two-beat structure. Jemaine's bluff → Bret's innocent deflation of it. 'I haven't actually read it yet, but I've got...' trailing off is the ideal ending — he can't even finish the sentence because there's nothing to add. Character-integrated: Bret's passive incompetence undermining Jemaine's attempt at bravado is central to their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: My rhymes are so potent that in this small segment / I made all the ladies in the area pregnant

jokes.text: John: 'Shut up, you English faggots, and give me the bike!'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine have been established as New Zealanders multiple times in the series
jokes.punchline: John calls them 'English' — conflating all English-speaking accents, likely because both sound vaguely British to American ears — which is both an insult and a geographic misidentification
jokes.explanation: The 'English' misidentification is a recurring joke in FOTC (Americans can't place the New Zealand accent). Works here as character comedy for John as well — he can't even insult them accurately. Functional rather than brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, sometimes my lyrics are sexist / But you lovely bitches now should know I'm trying to correct this

jokes.text: The Hiphopopotamus vs. Rhymenoceros rap song begins — Bret and Jemaine respond to a knife-point mugging by performing an elaborate hip-hop number
jokes.setup: Mickey has revealed a knife; the boys are in danger
jokes.punchline: Rather than complying or fighting, they break into a fully produced rap song — treating the mugging as a performance opportunity
jokes.explanation: The song sequence is the episode's central comic set piece and pays off the rap name introductions from the meeting scene. The premise — responding to a mugging with a rap performance — is completely absurd and completely in-character. It also serves as a callback to the band meeting where the names were introduced. The song's content is what generates the individual jokes (scored separately below).

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Other rappers diss me / They say my rhymes are sissy / Why? / Why? / Why exactly? / What... why? / Be more constructive with your feedback / Please, why?

jokes.text: # I'm the mother-flippin' Rhymenoceros / My beats are phat and the birds are on my back and I'm horny / I'm horny #
jokes.setup: Bret opens the rap with aggressive hip-hop posturing
jokes.punchline: 'I'm horny' — rhinos have horns; 'horny' means sexually aroused in common slang. Bret is using the literal rhinoceros meaning, which creates an inadvertent double meaning
jokes.explanation: Clean wordplay that earns its laugh. The word 'horny' in context of the Rhymenoceros persona is a natural pun — rhinos have horns — but it also delivers the double meaning immediately. The 'birds on my back' extension (rhinos often have oxpecker birds on them) shows the commitment to the rhino metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Why? 'Cause I rap about reality / Like me and my grandma drinking a cup of tea / There ain't no party like my nana's tea party

jokes.text: # I was raised by a rapper and writer... they dated / And subsequently procreated #
jokes.setup: Bret is delivering his rap origin story — establishing his credentials
jokes.punchline: 'Subsequently procreated' is hilariously clinical and un-rap for a birth announcement in a rap song — it sounds like a legal document or a biology textbook rather than hip-hop braggadocio
jokes.explanation: Excellent. The joke is the register mismatch — hip-hop is known for raw/slang language, and Bret's origin story uses the most sterile possible vocabulary. 'Subsequently procreated' is the kind of phrase used in academic papers about animal reproduction. It's also exactly how Bret would say things — he's earnest and slightly wrong. High originality; the specific word choice makes this.

dialogue.speaker: Mugger
dialogue.line: You guys dancing a little bit?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: # Where did you get that preposterous hypothesis? / Did Steve tell you that, perchance? / Mmm, Steve. #
jokes.setup: Jemaine has denied being a hippopotamus
jokes.punchline: He implies there is a specific person named Steve who has been spreading the hippopotamus allegation — 'Mmm, Steve' as a suspicious, knowing conclusion to an invented conspiracy
jokes.explanation: Tagged as a separate joke because it stands alone as a comedic idea. The 'Steve' bit adds a paranoid backstory to the hippopotamus denial — implying there is ongoing social conflict about Jemaine's mammal status. 'Mmm, Steve' is a perfect three-syllable button: suspicious, resigned, slightly threatening. Completely original. Extremely quotable. The word 'perchance' is another register mismatch that earns its keep.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That's not a knife.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's a knife.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, it is a knife.

jokes.text: # My rhymes and records, they don't get played / Because my records and rhymes, they don't get made / And if you rap like me, you don't get paid / And if you roll like me, you don't get laid #
jokes.setup: Jemaine is in the middle of a rap battle/threat, expected to boast about success
jokes.punchline: Four lines of honest self-deprecation that perfectly catalog Jemaine's real life: no airplay, no output, no income, no sex — delivered in the syntactic form of boasting
jokes.explanation: Arguably the sharpest writing in the episode. The four-line parallel structure ('if you rap like me / if you roll like me') is borrowed directly from hip-hop bragging conventions — but every line ends in failure instead of success. It's a perfect inversion: the form of a rap brag mapped onto a confession of total incompetence. The escalation from professional failure (no records made) to personal failure (no sex) is the right order. This is exactly as good as the '9.5 anchor' in structural sophistication if not in cultural impact.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, my sleeve just... help me, my sleeve's just caught on the fence.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm too scared, man.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, the corduroy's caught on the fence, Bret.

jokes.text: # My rhymes are so potent that in this small segment / I made all of the ladies in the area pregnant / Yes, sometimes my lyrics are sexist / But you lovely bitches now should know I'm trying to correct this #
jokes.setup: Jemaine has just admitted he doesn't get laid; now claims supernatural sexual power through rhyme
jokes.punchline: He then immediately acknowledges the sexism of this claim and apologizes for it — but calls women 'bitches' in the apology, defeating the apology entirely
jokes.explanation: Exceptional writing. The joke operates on three levels: (1) the absurd claim of impregnating ladies through rhyme, (2) the meta-awareness that the lyric is sexist — breaking the fourth wall within the song itself, (3) the self-undermining apology that uses 'lovely bitches' — which is still sexist. It's a miniature deconstruction of hip-hop's complicated relationship with misogyny, executed with perfect economy. The word 'segment' instead of 'verse' is also a nice register joke.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There was two of them.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Two? That's not a gang.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It was a two-man gang.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Two-man gang.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Can you have that, David?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Well that's a pretty small gang. I mean, technically, the smallest gang possible.

jokes.text: # Other rappers diss me / They say my rhymes are sissy / Why? / Why? / Why exactly? / What... why? / Be more constructive with your feedback / Please, why? #
jokes.setup: Jemaine claims other rappers diss him for being sissy
jokes.punchline: Rather than responding with aggression (as rap convention demands), he responds with genuine bewilderment and a request for specific, constructive criticism — asking 'why?' repeatedly in an increasingly plaintive way
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of rap battle conventions. The response to being dissed should be a counter-diss; instead Jemaine wants actionable feedback. 'Be more constructive with your feedback' in a rap context is both funny and revealing of character — Jemaine is fundamentally a sensitive person trying to do rap. The escalating 'Why? Why? Why exactly? What... why?' is a perfect comedic rhythm. Extremely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You just left him there to fight them himself?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, I left him on the... he was on the fence.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Bret!

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, I was... I was very scared.

jokes.text: # 'Cause I rap about reality / Like me and my grandma drinking a cup of tea / There ain't no party like my nana's tea party / Hi-ho #
jokes.setup: Jemaine defends rapping about 'reality'
jokes.punchline: His example of 'reality rap' is having tea with his grandmother — delivered in gangsta rap cadence as if this is gritty street content. 'Hi-ho' as a tag is the final gentle absurdism.
jokes.explanation: Iconic FOTC moment. The entire joke rests on the collision between hip-hop form ('I rap about reality') and content (grandma's tea party). 'There ain't no party like my nana's tea party' is a perfect line — it uses the hip-hop 'there ain't no party like...' formula but substitutes the rowdy party for a domestic ritual with an elderly relative. 'Hi-ho' as the signoff is the correct level of gentle absurdism. High quotability, strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He may be dead.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: He maybe did what?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He may be dead.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I know, but what did he maybe do?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He may be dead.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Are you guys fucking with me?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Not alive. You know... dead.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh. Oh.

jokes.text: After the song ends: 'You guys dancing a little bit?' / 'No.' / 'I told you to give us the bikes, you didn't listen, so now this is happening.'
jokes.setup: The song has ended; the muggers have presumably been standing there for two minutes while Bret and Jemaine rap at them
jokes.punchline: John noticed the boys were swaying/dancing and asks about it; Bret denies it; John frames the entire rap performance as a consequence of not complying — 'you didn't listen, so now this is happening'
jokes.explanation: Excellent button on the song. John's 'this is happening' — referring to the rap performance as an unwanted event inflicted on them — reframes the entire sequence from the muggers' perspective. They didn't ask for a concert. 'You didn't listen, so now this is happening' has a tired parental tone that's perfect. The reaction beat (the implied awkward silence of two muggers watching a rap number) is the real setup.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I can't believe that he just ran off like that when the cops came and left me there.

jokes.text: Mickey reveals his knife — 'That's not a knife' / 'Yeah, that's a knife' / 'Oh, it is a knife'
jokes.setup: John orders Mickey to show them the knife as a threat
jokes.punchline: Bret's immediate reaction is that it's not a knife — then he reconsiders and accepts it is. The pause between denial and acceptance implies the knife was either unexpectedly large or small, either way subverting the expected reaction
jokes.explanation: Possible Crocodile Dundee reference (the 'that's not a knife' scene is famous). If intentional, it's a meta-joke about antipodean culture. Even without the reference, the beat works as visual comedy — the implied visual of the knife is the punchline. Economy is strong.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You... kill people?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Maybe.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Why, you ever kill somebody?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Not really.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Yeah, me neither.

jokes.text: Mid-chase: 'Uh, Bret, my sleeve just... help me, my sleeve's just caught on the fence.' / 'I'm too scared, man.' / 'Bret, the corduroy's caught on the fence, Bret.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine are fleeing the knife-wielding muggers
jokes.punchline: Jemaine gets his corduroy jacket caught on a fence — the texture of his fabric choice is the operative detail — and Bret refuses to help because he's scared, leaving Jemaine stuck
jokes.explanation: Character-integrated physical comedy. The specificity of 'corduroy' is perfect — it's such an unheroic fabric to be betrayed by during a chase. 'Bret, the corduroy's caught on the fence, Bret' as an urgent plea manages to be both desperate and faintly absurd. This also plants the abandonment conflict that drives the second half of the episode.

dialogue.line: Killed a monkey once.
Mmm. Uh-huh.
Don't tell anybody about that, okay? 'Cause I'm not proud of it.

jokes.text: Bret: 'There was two of them.' / Dave: 'Two? That's not a gang.' / Bret: 'It was a two-man gang.' / Dave: 'I mean, technically, the smallest gang possible.'
jokes.setup: Bret describes being surrounded by a 'gang' after the mugging
jokes.punchline: Dave points out two people is not a gang; Bret insists on the 'two-man gang' designation; Dave adjudicates that it's 'technically, the smallest gang possible'
jokes.explanation: Clean three-beat exchange. The taxonomic debate about minimum gang size is funny because everyone treats it seriously. 'Technically, the smallest gang possible' is the correct deadpan authority for Dave — he's not dismissing Bret's experience, just correcting his terminology.

dialogue.line: Hey guys, remember that time he got his hand caught in that jar?
What a dick. I mean, who gets their hand caught in a jar?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That was... that was actually me.
That was Bret.

jokes.text: Murray: 'A lot of New Zealanders come over here and they come into my office, and I give them the vest, a map, and I tell them to stay clear of crowds by going through back alleys. Yet almost every day a New Zealander is mugged.'
jokes.setup: Murray is processing the mugging and explaining his safety protocols
jokes.punchline: Murray advises people to 'stay clear of crowds by going through back alleys' — which is worse advice, since back alleys are where muggings happen — and then wonders why muggings still occur despite this excellent guidance
jokes.explanation: Calls back to Murray's safety equipment distribution. The callback reveals a systematic pattern: Murray has been advising a whole population of New Zealanders using backwards logic. 'Almost every day a New Zealander is mugged' as a result of his specific advice is the payoff. Extremely character-integrated — only Murray would both distribute the safety vest program AND advise back alley navigation.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Jemaine was the one who helped you out. And he helped you when you got your head stuck in the chair.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Remember?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Yeah. You were there for hours.

jokes.text: Murray: 'He may be dead.' / Bret: 'He maybe did what?' / Murray: 'He may be dead.' / Bret: 'I know, but what did he maybe do?' — repeated three times
jokes.setup: Murray is expressing concern that Jemaine might be dead; Bret keeps mishearing 'may be dead' as 'maybe did'
jokes.punchline: The phonetic similarity between 'may be dead' and 'maybe did' creates a loop where Murray and Bret talk past each other — Murray trying to convey mortality, Bret trying to find out what action Jemaine 'maybe did' — culminating in Dave asking 'Are you guys fucking with me?'
jokes.explanation: Good phonetic misunderstanding joke that escalates through repetition. The triple repeat is structurally sound (comedy rule of three). Dave's exasperated 'Are you guys fucking with me?' is the correct button — he can't tell if this is a bit or genuine confusion. The misunderstanding works because it's plausible with accents.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: You know, I never understood a fucking word that guy said.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He did say 'what' a lot.

jokes.text: Talking head: Jemaine: 'I can't believe that he just ran off like that when the cops came and left me there.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine was left stuck on a fence by Bret
jokes.punchline: New detail: Bret ran when the cops came — not just when the muggers came. This reveals Bret ran from both threats, making him equally afraid of police and muggers
jokes.explanation: The detail that Bret also fled from the cops adds a layer — was Bret always going to run from everyone? It implies Bret's flight response is general rather than specifically cowardly in this case. Functional setup for the abandonment theme.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: So I got to untie the guy, right? I got to apologize. He's bleeding all over the place, I got to help him. It was very awkward shit. I was very upset about it.

jokes.text: Bret talking head: 'That kind of thing, it defines character.' / [pause] / 'Yeah.' / 'Son of a bitch.' / 'Yeah, you know, 'cause you were deserted too.'
jokes.setup: Bret is presumably agreeing that abandoning someone defines character — but it applies to himself
jokes.punchline: Dave's validation 'Son of a bitch' and then connecting Bret's abandonment to some past experience of being deserted — Bret condemns himself without realizing it, or knowingly applies the standard to himself
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Bret-John parallel storyline. The comedic irony is functional but not especially sharp. Works better as plot mechanics.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, one time we were supposed to go to the movies, and... uh, 'March of the Penguins.' And he... I had bought the tickets, and he didn't turn up. He texted me, said, 'I'm not coming.'

jokes.text: John begins his story: mugger explaining he had to shoot someone in the kneecap on instruction, then got a text 45 minutes later saying 'call it off'
jokes.setup: Jemaine and John are bonding over being abandoned; John starts what sounds like a relatable tale
jokes.punchline: John's abandonment story involves shooting someone, kneecapping them, then having to apologize while they're bleeding — presented as a workplace miscommunication complaint
jokes.explanation: The joke is the parallel structure: Jemaine and John bonding over being let down by their partners, but John's version involves kneecap-shooting. The framing as a professional grievance ('a text, mind you, not a phone call') treats violent crime as ordinary workplace frustration. 'He was bleeding all over the place, I got to help him. It was very awkward shit' is the perfect deadpan note — John is most upset about the awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, one time we were supposed to go to the movies, and... uh, 'March of the Penguins.'

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Oh, I love that movie.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: And, he... I had bought the tickets, and he didn't turn up. He texted me, said, 'I'm not coming.'

jokes.text: John: 'Killed a monkey once.' / [pause] / 'Don't tell anybody about that, okay? 'Cause I'm not proud of it.'
jokes.setup: John and Jemaine are bonding; John is listing things he has and hasn't killed
jokes.punchline: After establishing he hasn't killed anyone, John admits he's killed a monkey — delivered with genuine remorse and a request for secrecy
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best standalone jokes. The comedic mechanism is the specificity — not a person, not a crime, but a monkey. John's genuine shame ('I'm not proud of it') is what makes it land: this isn't presented as threatening, it's a regret. The request for secrecy about monkey murder to someone he just met, delivered in the same tone as any embarrassing confession, is perfectly absurd. Also seeds the callback in id:67 and the finale.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Well, that's... that's not really the same thing, is it? I mean... I... I shot a guy, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yours is worse.

jokes.text: Dave: 'Hey guys, remember that time he got his hand caught in that jar? What a dick. I mean, who gets their hand caught in a jar? What a big fucking asshole he was.' / 'That was actually me.' / 'That was Bret, David.'
jokes.setup: Everyone is eulogizing Jemaine in case he's dead, sharing memories
jokes.punchline: Dave's 'hilarious' Jemaine memory is apparently Bret's — the story was always about Bret, not Jemaine. Dave has been insulting Bret to his face thinking it was a Jemaine story.
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection/reveal structure. Dave builds with increasing aggression toward 'Jemaine' ('what a dick,' 'big fucking asshole') and then the rug is pulled. Bret's mild 'that was actually me' rather than an offended response is perfect — he doesn't take the insults personally, just corrects the attribution. Murray's matter-of-fact 'That was Bret, David' is the correct delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Kind of sounds like, 'What? What, what? Don't poot me in there weeth him. I'm innocent. I'm innocent'?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yes, that's definitely him.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's him.
It's a little bit more sort of South African.

jokes.text: Dave: 'And he helped you when you got your head stuck in the chair. Remember? You were there for hours.'
jokes.setup: Murray is now being reminded of times Jemaine helped him
jokes.punchline: Murray got his head stuck in a chair and was there for 'hours' — a ridiculous predicament, and the extended duration makes it worse
jokes.explanation: A quick joke that does double work: establishes Jemaine's helpfulness (setting up the abandonment theme) and reveals Murray has his own history of absurd mishaps. 'Hours' is the correct duration for comedic escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: 'Cause I rented your half of the apartment out to Rueben?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Does he have to stay here still?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, he's paid two weeks' rent, 'cause I didn't think you were coming back.

jokes.text: Dave: 'Hey, what was that one thing he used to say? That always cracked me up. He'd be like... What? What? What?' / 'I never understood a fucking word that guy said.' / 'He did say what a lot.'
jokes.setup: Dave wants to share a memorable Jemaine quote as part of the eulogy
jokes.punchline: The most memorable thing Jemaine 'used to say' is just 'What? What?' — and Dave didn't understand a word Jemaine said, despite knowing him well. Murray's earnest analysis 'He was just always questioning' reframes confusion as a philosophical trait.
jokes.explanation: Excellent blend of character comedy and New Zealand accent joke. Dave's fondest Jemaine memory is incomprehension; Murray reframes this incomprehension as Jemaine being 'questioning' and 'loyal.' The transition from 'What?' to the philosophical 'He was just always questioning' is a beautiful comedic pivot.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I wouldn't have done it if I'd known you were gonna hassle me about it.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You shouldn't have done it anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hassling me.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm not hassling you.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You are hassling me.

jokes.text: Jemaine comparing his experience to John's: both about being texted instead of called. Jemaine: 'He texted me, said I'm not coming' [to March of the Penguins]. / John: 'Well, that's not really the same thing, is it? I... I shot a guy.' / Jemaine: '...Yours is worse.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine draws a parallel between John's story (texted after kneecapping someone) and his own (texted when Bret didn't show up to see March of the Penguins)
jokes.punchline: John correctly identifies these are not equivalent; Jemaine acknowledges this with 'Yours is worse' — an extraordinary understatement about kneecap-shooting being 'worse' than a cancelled movie outing
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan understatement payoff to the parallel stories setup. 'Yours is worse' — applied to violent crime vs. a movie date — is hilariously inadequate. The timing is key: Jemaine doesn't arrive at this conclusion quickly; he has to consider whether John's experience really is worse. The fact that 'March of the Penguins' was specifically chosen as Jemaine's example adds further deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Okay, David Hassle-hoff.

jokes.text: Police officer describing the found person: 'Kind of talks in a monotone, sounds like a robot?'
jokes.setup: The police are describing a found person who might be Jemaine
jokes.punchline: The New Zealand accent described as 'sounds like a robot' — a flat, deadpan intonation pattern that sounds inhuman to American ears
jokes.explanation: Good New Zealand accent joke. Murray's response — 'Well, that's the New Zealand accent' — is the correct flat defense. The robot description captures something true about the FOTC deadpan delivery style.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Did the cops try to strip search you?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Did they find anything?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, they didn't strip search me.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, I... I thought you said they did.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Police officer doing Jemaine's accent: 'Don't poot me in there weeth him. I'm innocent. I'm innocent.' / Murray: 'Yes, that's definitely him.' / Bret: 'It's a little bit more sort of South African.'
jokes.setup: The officer performs the found person's accent for identification
jokes.punchline: Murray immediately confirms it's Jemaine; Bret gently corrects the accent impression — 'a little bit more sort of South African' — splitting the difference on exactly how wrong the impersonation is
jokes.explanation: Bret's gentle correction is the key beat. He doesn't deny it's Jemaine, but he has professional standards about accent accuracy even in this context. The willingness to split hairs about the quality of the impression is perfect Bret behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, did any of your cellmates, you know, rape you in the a...

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No. No no no.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh good, good. 'Cause if... if I was a convict, you know, and I was in a cell with a pretty boy like you, I would definitely wait till lights out, put my hand over your mouth...

jokes.text: Bret: 'Is she gonna bring the body out here?'
jokes.setup: The officer has said they found someone matching Jemaine's description; Murray says they need to see him 'visually'
jokes.punchline: Bret immediately assumes Jemaine is dead and asks if the body will be brought to them — while the others have presumably kept open the possibility that Jemaine is alive
jokes.explanation: Quick, dry joke. Bret has fully committed to the 'he's dead' scenario — asking about the body retrieval logistics. The casualness is the comedy. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Being in jail just makes you seem so sexy. I guess it's the danger aspect. What are you gonna do to me? Do it! No, don't, don't.

jokes.text: Bret: 'I wouldn't have done it if I'd known you were gonna hassle me about it.' / Jemaine: 'You shouldn't have done it anyway.' / Bret: 'Hassling me.' / Jemaine: 'I'm not hassling you...' / Bret: 'Hassling me.' / Jemaine: 'Height of rudeness.' / Bret: 'Okay, David Hassle-hoff.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine are arguing about the abandonment; Bret objects to being 'hassled'
jokes.punchline: Bret ends the argument by calling Jemaine 'David Hassle-hoff' — a pun on 'hassle' and David Hasselhoff — which is both a pun and a way of refusing to engage with the argument further
jokes.explanation: The pun lands because Bret has been repeating 'hassling me' like an incantation, so when 'Hassle-hoff' arrives it feels earned. It's also a comedic escape from the argument — Bret can't win on the merits, so he puns his way out. Character-integrated: Bret is the type who would deploy a pun to end an uncomfortable conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's like Bonnie and Clyde, you know. They would rob banks, and then they would do each other.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Oh, I thought you said they did [strip search you].' / 'Well, did any of your cellmates... good. 'Cause if I was a convict and I was in a cell with a pretty boy like you, I would definitely wait till lights out, put my hand over your mouth...'
jokes.setup: Murray is expressing relief that Jemaine wasn't harmed in jail
jokes.punchline: Murray's 'relief' expression involves describing in detail what he personally would have done to Jemaine in prison — which is both wildly inappropriate and reveals Murray has thought about this in some detail
jokes.explanation: Murray's complete unawareness that describing his own hypothetical assault on Jemaine is inappropriate is funny in itself. The fact that he's trying to express sympathy while doing this is what makes it cringe-comedy rather than just dark.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's not a real gangster name.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: And Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, I've been on the inside, I know what sort of names gangsters have. Like John. There was another guy there... Ralph.

jokes.text: Mel: 'Did the cops try to strip search you?' / 'Did any of your cellmates, you know, rape you in the a—' (cut off by Jemaine's horrified departure)
jokes.setup: Mel is greeting the recently released Jemaine with concern
jokes.punchline: Her 'concern' rapidly escalates through strip searches to asking about prison rape — with a tone that suggests genuine curiosity rather than concern — until Jemaine cuts her off and leaves
jokes.explanation: The escalation from normal concern to deeply inappropriate questions is the key structure. Mel's obliviousness to why these questions are wrong is perfectly calibrated — she's not malicious, just without social filter. The incomplete 'in the a—' is funnier than the complete question would be.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Um, Rhymenoceros. Rhymenoceros, yep. That's not a real gangster name.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What? And Murray. Well, I've been on the inside, I know what sort of names gangsters have. Like John. There was another guy there... Ralph.

jokes.text: Mel: 'It's like Bonnie and Clyde, you know. They would rob banks, and then they would do each other.'
jokes.setup: Mel is projecting a romantic outlaw fantasy onto Jemaine's two-day jail stint
jokes.punchline: 'They would do each other' — Mel's casually sexual framing of the Bonnie and Clyde relationship, said to Jemaine in front of Murray
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the Mel-as-obsessive-fan character beat. 'They would do each other' is the casual indelicacy that defines Mel's social obliviousness. She's not trying to be provocative; she just doesn't notice appropriate boundaries.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, you were only in there for two days, man.

jokes.text: Mel: 'What are you gonna do to me? Do it! No, don't, don't.'
jokes.setup: Mel is projecting a dangerous outlaw persona onto Jemaine
jokes.punchline: She provokes him ('What are you gonna do to me? Do it!') then immediately panics and reverses ('No, don't, don't') — the reversal happens too fast, revealing her ambivalence and general inappropriateness
jokes.explanation: Good Mel character beat. The self-contradiction within a single breath ('Do it! No, don't, don't') is a compact expression of her dysfunction. Works as escalation within the Mel-jailbird-fantasy sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Sorry, if I can't trust him on the streets, I can't trust him on the stage. I can't do another gig with him without going against everything I stand for.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, what if you actually had another gig?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, then I would.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Oh, yeah, just run, Bret. Just run. It's like Bonnie and Clyde, you know. They would rob banks, and then they would do each other.'
jokes.setup: Bret has fled the Mel situation; Murray calls after him
jokes.punchline: Murray repeats Mel's Bonnie and Clyde reference verbatim — either parroting it because he heard it, or genuinely arriving at the same conclusion independently
jokes.explanation: Murray adopting Mel's weird Bonnie and Clyde framing adds another beat to their shared obliviousness. Works better as a callback than as a standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: But to be fair to Bret, John, you were one of those hoodlums mugging us, you know?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's true.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I guess I am somewhat to blame as well, huh?

jokes.text: Band meeting: Jemaine claims prison authority on gangster names — 'I've been on the inside, I know what sort of names gangsters have.' / 'Like John.' / 'There was another guy there... Ralph.'
jokes.setup: Murray has dismissed 'Rhymenoceros' as not a real gangster name; Jemaine appeals to his two-day prison experience as authority
jokes.punchline: The gangster names from Jemaine's prison experience are 'John' and 'Ralph' — completely ordinary names that suggest either Jemaine was in the world's most mundane prison or he didn't meet any notable criminals
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The setup promises exotic criminal monikers — 'I know what gangsters are called' — and delivers 'John' and 'Ralph.' The escalation structure (first John, then Ralph as a second example) is perfect. 'There was another guy there... Ralph' has the exact right cadence — 'another guy' implies many to choose from, and Ralph is the worst possible gangster name. Strong character integration: Jemaine genuinely thinks 48 hours inside qualifies him as a criminal authority.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: At least I didn't kill a monkey.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Dawg.

jokes.text: Band meeting: Jemaine claims his jail experience gives him insight into gangster names: 'Well, I've been on the inside, I know what sort of names gangsters have. Like John. There was another guy there... Ralph.'
jokes.setup: Murray is skeptical of Jemaine's authority on gangster names after two days in jail.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's evidence of authentic gangster names from 'the inside' is 'John' and 'Ralph' — two of the least threatening names imaginable.
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's two days in holding have given him enormous confidence in his criminal expertise. 'Like John. There was another guy there... Ralph.' The specificity of Ralph after John is perfect — both names are maximum-benign. Jemaine's confidence in offering these as examples of authentic street credentials is total character integration. The callback to John (who is, of course, one of the muggers) adds a layer.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Brought you a cup of tea, man.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, thank you, Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Please grab it, it's hot.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It's hot.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Deal with it yourself. That's what I've learned to do the hard way. Oh, ow ow ow... oh, fuck! Ow.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'If I can't trust him on the streets, I can't trust him on the stage. I can't do another gig with him without going against everything I stand for.' / Murray: 'Well, what if you actually had another gig?' / Jemaine: 'Well, then I would.' / Murray: 'I haven't actually got you one, but that's good to know.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine makes a dramatic declaration about refusing to perform with Bret
jokes.punchline: Murray hypothetically offers a gig to test Jemaine's resolve; Jemaine immediately folds — he would do the gig; Murray reveals he doesn't actually have a gig, deflating everything, then adds 'but that's good to know'
jokes.explanation: One of the best exchanges in the episode. The structure is perfect: dramatic declaration → hypothetical test → instant capitulation → reveal the test was hypothetical → 'but that's good to know.' Murray's 'I haven't actually got you one' is beautifully timed, and the coda 'but that's good to know' transforms the whole exchange into a revelation about Jemaine's principles (they cost nothing to maintain when untested). Also callbacks to the running gig joke. Both character voices are pitch-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hey, Jemaine, I'll see you later, maybe.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bye, Bret.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: If I live, that is, 'cause I might get killed.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Okay, let me know how that goes.

jokes.text: Murray: 'What's this meeting about?' / 'Well, one of my CDs is missing.'
jokes.setup: The band meeting has been nominally about the Bret-Jemaine conflict and band trust
jokes.punchline: Murray's actual agenda item for the meeting — the reason he called it — is a missing CD, mentioned as a complete non-sequitur after the emotional conflict has been aired
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation ending to a dramatic scene. Murray has been managing a genuine interpersonal crisis — and the entire meeting was actually called about a missing CD. The bathos is excellent and character-integrated: Murray's priorities are always slightly wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, I didn't really love that camera phone as much as you thought I loved it. In fact, I kind of think you ruined my phone when you made me that camera phone. And my camera.

jokes.text: John empathizing with Jemaine about Bret's abandonment — 'So your friend... your best friend in the whole world... just left you there with a bunch of crazy-ass muggers?' — while being one of those muggers
jokes.setup: John is showing sympathy for Jemaine being abandoned during the mugging
jokes.punchline: John is one of the people who mugged Jemaine — his sympathy is entirely self-unaware. He's commiserating about the mugging he committed.
jokes.explanation: The irony is the joke. John expressing outrage at Jemaine's abandonment by 'crazy-ass muggers' — he IS a crazy-ass mugger who was also there. The show commits to this without telegraphing it; John and Jemaine have genuinely bonded. The absurdity of the situation accumulates.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I didn't really love that camera phone as much as you thought I loved it. In fact, I kind of think you ruined my phone when you made me that camera phone. And my camera.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'But to be fair to Bret, John, you were one of those hoodlums mugging us, you know?'
jokes.setup: John has been empathetically condemning Bret's abandonment of Jemaine to muggers
jokes.punchline: Jemaine gently points out that John was one of the muggers — said in a 'to be fair' tone, as if this is a minor contextual correction rather than a devastating point
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan. 'To be fair to Bret, John, you were one of those hoodlums mugging us' — the register is so calm, so balanced, using 'hoodlums' as a quaint qualifier. Jemaine isn't angry about this; he's just making sure the record is accurate. The phrasing 'to be fair to Bret' treats the mugging as a mitigating factor in the abandonment debate rather than as the central moral issue. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's children on the street using guns and knives / Taking drugs and each other's lives / Killing each other with knives and forks / Calling each other names like dork

jokes.text: John: 'Yeah, that's true. I guess I am somewhat to blame as well, huh? But it's still shitty, what he did.' / Jemaine: 'Yes, I agree with that.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has just noted John was one of the muggers
jokes.punchline: John accepts partial blame ('somewhat to blame') for mugging Jemaine, then immediately pivots to reaffirming that Bret's abandonment was worse — and Jemaine agrees, completing the bizarre moral calculus
jokes.explanation: Jemaine and John have arrived at a consensus that John mugging Jemaine is less bad than Bret abandoning him. 'Somewhat to blame' for armed robbery is perfect understatement. The mutual agreement seals the absurd moral framework they've constructed together. Strong structural payoff.

dialogue.line: Who's touching these monkeys?
Please leave these poor sick monkeys alone

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'At least I didn't kill a monkey.' / [pause] / John: 'Dawg.'
jokes.setup: John has been judging Bret and establishing his own moral authority
jokes.punchline: Jemaine deploys the monkey killing as a mic-drop; John's single-word response 'Dawg' — a street-affirmation that also implies 'touché/fair enough' — is perfectly deflated
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff. The monkey murder was introduced as an unsettling aside in an earlier scene; here it functions as Jemaine's moral trump card. 'Dawg' as John's response is extraordinary — he accepts the rebuttal, and the single word does enormous work. The pause before 'Dawg' is the joke. Economy score is near-perfect. The callback reveals the monkey detail was planted specifically for this moment.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's people on the street getting diseases from monkeys
Yeah, that's what I said, they're getting diseases from monkeys

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Now there's junkies with monkey disease
Who's touching these monkeys?

jokes.text: Bret tries to apologize with hot tea → burns himself badly → Jemaine: 'I guess we're even then.'
jokes.setup: Bret brings Jemaine tea as a peace offering; Jemaine refuses to take it, so Bret holds it
jokes.punchline: Bret burns himself badly on the hot tea; Jemaine decides this constitutes fair punishment and declares them even
jokes.explanation: Satisfying physical payoff. The moral calculus — Bret burning himself on tea equals Bret abandoning Jemaine to knife-wielding muggers — is absurd but Jemaine's delivery of 'I guess we're even then' is perfect. The implicit score-keeping is character-integrated for Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Why are we still paying so much for sneakers when they're made by little slave kids? What are your overheads?

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'I'm not hungry!' — refusing Bret's pizza olive branch
jokes.setup: Bret has brought tea (which burnt him), now brings pizza as a second reconciliation attempt
jokes.punchline: Jemaine has just said they're even, then immediately refuses the pizza — suggesting he's not done being difficult despite the declared truce
jokes.explanation: Quick beat that undermines the resolution — Jemaine isn't fully ready to make peace despite declaring them 'even.' Functional, not exceptional. The 'I'm not hungry!' delivered off-screen while Bret is already burnt suggests Jemaine is petulant more than angry.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Saw a man lying on the street half-dead with knives and forks sticking out of his leg
He said, 'Ah ah ah, ow ow ow ow ow ow
Can somebody get the knife and fork out of my leg please?'
'Can somebody please remove these cutleries from my knees?'

jokes.text: Bret going to retrieve the camera phone from the mugger by approaching Mickey directly and identifying himself: 'Yeah yeah. The one that ran away.'
jokes.setup: Bret approaches Mickey to ask for the camera phone back
jokes.punchline: Mickey identifies Bret immediately as 'the one that ran away' — Bret's most defining characteristic from the mugging is his cowardice, and even the mugger catalogued him this way
jokes.explanation: Good callback. Bret is immortalized in Mickey's memory specifically for running away — not for his music, his personality, or any positive trait. 'Yeah yeah. The one that ran away.' as Mickey's immediate recognition confirms the episode's central joke about Bret's character.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Here are the photos... I got 'em developed. From the camera phone.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You guys had some really good times, huh?

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: I hope you don't mind, there's some of me in there. I had to finish out the roll.

jokes.text: Bret's dramatic farewell: 'If I die, I want you to tell my mom.' / Jemaine: 'Okay. Well, write... write down her address.' / Bret: 'Okay.' [starts writing] / Jemaine: 'Well, just phone... you can just phone her and tell her.' / Bret: 'Oh, okay, I'll just phone her.'
jokes.setup: Bret is departing on what he frames as a dangerous mission to retrieve the camera phone
jokes.punchline: His solemn dying request (tell my mom) is immediately reduced to a logistics conversation about whether to write the address or just phone — Jemaine's practical suggestion deflates the drama entirely
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the opening phone call scene — now it's Bret contemplating his mom being called with death news, and Jemaine suggests just phoning her as if this is a normal errand. The deflation of Bret's dramatic gesture into a simple telephone logistics question is perfectly FOTC. The callback to the opening scene (phoning mom) is likely intentional. Character-integrated: Jemaine's pragmatism kills Bret's drama.

dialogue.speaker: Mickey
dialogue.line: I just wish John was big enough to accept my apology, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well man, we're gonna get some pizza. Do you want to come and get a slice of pizza or something?

dialogue.speaker: Mickey
dialogue.line: Yeah? Really? Oh, I'd love to, man.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'You don't need to go and do that.' / Bret: 'Yes, I do.' / Jemaine: 'No, you don't.' / Bret: 'Well, you loved that thing.' / Jemaine: 'Yeah, I didn't really love that camera phone as much as you thought I loved it. In fact, I kind of think you ruined my phone when you made me that camera phone. And my camera.'
jokes.setup: Bret is risking himself to recover the camera phone because Jemaine loved it so much
jokes.punchline: Jemaine reveals he never actually loved the camera phone — the gift ruined both the phone and the camera it was glued to — deflating Bret's heroic gesture at its foundation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff to the camera phone thread. The entire second half of the episode has been Bret's redemption arc — risking himself to recover a gift for Jemaine — and this reveal pulls the rug: the gift was bad and Jemaine knew it. 'I kind of think you ruined my phone when you made me that camera phone. And my camera.' is the key line — the gift destroyed two items. John the mugger was right ('it's a piece of shit'). Outstanding callback structure with multiple levels of irony.

dialogue.speaker: Mickey
dialogue.line: Um, I'm going to a White Supremacist gig. It's gonna be awesome.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, we'll probably just get the pizza.

jokes.text: The 'Inner City Pressure' song begins.
jokes.setup: Bret walks out into the city streets to confront the mugger.
jokes.punchline: The episode's second musical number — a Pet Shop Boys parody ('West End Girls') about urban poverty performed by two semi-homeless musicians.
jokes.explanation: The second major musical set piece. The conceit — a synth-pop anthem about inner city pressure performed by two New Zealanders who are poor in a very minor way — is inspired. The genre choice (Pet Shop Boys pastiche) is perfect: a synth-pop anthem about urban poverty by two slightly uncomfortable white guys. The song functions as a character study and comedic accumulation of their specific, undramatic problems.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: Man, this whole week's been terrible. I haven't felt this bad since Cheeky died. Man, I miss that monkey.

jokes.text: Lyric: '# There's people on the street getting diseases from monkeys / Yeah, that's what I said, they're getting diseases from monkeys / Now there's junkies with monkey disease / Who's touching these monkeys? / P-lease leave these poor sick monkeys alone / They've got problems enough as it is #'
jokes.setup: The social commentary escalates from violence to disease.
jokes.punchline: The monkey disease tangent derails the song entirely — 'who's touching these monkeys?' and 'they've got problems enough as it is' pivot the concern from the junkies to the monkeys, then from monkey health to monkey problems.
jokes.explanation: The monkey disease section is outstanding for several reasons: (1) it's absurdist social commentary that follows its own internal logic, (2) the concern pivots from the junkies to the monkeys themselves, (3) 'they've got problems enough as it is' implies sympathy for sick monkeys as the primary victims, and (4) it's a callback to John's monkey confession, adding a dark subtext. 'Who's touching these monkeys? P-lease' is an incredibly funny register — exasperated and slightly accusatory. One of the song's best moments.

jokes.text: Lyric: '# Man is lying on the street, some punk's chopped off his head / I'm the only one who stops to see if he's dead / Mmm, turns out he's dead #'
jokes.setup: The song builds toward an act of civic engagement — stopping to help someone in need.
jokes.punchline: 'Mmm, turns out he's dead' — the investigation confirms the obvious, delivered with mild disappointment.
jokes.explanation: This might be the single best line in the episode. 'Mmm, turns out he's dead' is a masterclass in deadpan. The setup — head chopped off, singer is the only one who stops — creates an expectation of heroism or at least useful intervention. The punchline is not only that the man is dead (obviously) but the 'Mmm' — a brief, thoughtful consideration — and 'turns out' as if the conclusion of the investigation surprised no one more than the singer. The economy is perfect: five words that generate enormous laughter. This is comparable to the 9.5 anchor example in construction and impact.

jokes.text: Lyric: '# Good cops being framed / And put into a can / All the money that we're making / It's going to the Man / What man? Which man? Who's the man? / When's a man a man? What makes a man a man? / Am I a man? Yes / Technically, I am #'
jokes.setup: Social commentary about systemic oppression — money going to 'the Man.'
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'the Man' triggers a genuine philosophical inquiry about manhood, resolved only by 'Yes. Technically, I am' — the most minimal possible confirmation of masculine identity.
jokes.explanation: Another exceptional lyric. The escalating questions — 'What man? Which man? Who's the man? When's a man a man? What makes a man a man? Am I a man?' — build to genuine existential crisis, then resolve with 'Yes. Technically, I am.' The word 'technically' is doing all the work: it implies the singer is a man only by strict definition, with reservations, with acknowledgment that the category barely applies. This is brilliant character writing — self-doubt embedded in a social commentary anthem. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor example and above.

jokes.text: Song lyric: 'Saw a man lying on the street half-dead / With knives and forks sticking out of his leg / He said, Ah ah ah, ow ow ow ow / Can somebody get the knife and fork out of my leg please? / Can somebody please remove these cutleries from my knees?'
jokes.setup: A callback to 'knives and forks' from the opening verse.
jokes.punchline: The song returns to its cutlery motif with a specific victim — knives AND forks lodged in a leg. The victim's polite, specific request ('Can somebody please remove these cutleries from my knees?') and the grammatically uncertain 'cutleries' is perfectly absurd.
jokes.explanation: 'Cutleries' as the plural is an inspired malapropism that makes the line funnier. The callback structure — establishing 'knives and forks' as a weapon early, then revisiting it with a specific victim who politely requests their removal — is excellent songwriting as comedy architecture. 'From my knees' also mislabels the leg. The victim's formality ('please remove') in crisis is perfect.

jokes.text: Lyric: '# Saw a man lying on the street half-dead / With knives and forks sticking out of his leg / He said, "Ah ah ah, ow ow ow ow ow ow / Can somebody get the knife and fork out of my leg please?" / "Can somebody please remove these cutleries from my knees?" #'
jokes.setup: Callback to the 'knives and forks' lyric from the opening verse.
jokes.punchline: The earlier 'killing each other with knives and forks' is now literalized — a man with cutlery embedded in his leg. 'Remove these cutleries from my knees' is the formal complaint.
jokes.explanation: The callback structure is excellent — the opening verse mentioned 'knives and forks' as a throwaway absurdity, now they've committed to it with a full scene. The pain sounds ('ah ah ah, ow ow ow') followed by the formal register of 'can somebody please remove these cutleries from my knees?' — 'cutleries' as the plural of cutlery (slightly wrong) and the very formal 'from my knees' — is a wonderful contrast. The victim is polite even in severe distress.

jokes.text: Mickey (mugger) returns the camera phone with developed photos — including photos of himself on a roller coaster and 'me with a shopkeeper, pointing a gun. Actually, I should take that.'
jokes.setup: Mickey returns the camera phone and developed photos.
jokes.punchline: Mickey used the camera phone's film for his own memories, including incriminating evidence (pointing a gun at a shopkeeper). He casually reclaims the crime photo.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback to the camera phone with added comedy: Mickey used it as a personal camera, roller coaster and armed robbery alike. The casual recovery of crime-scene evidence is a great character beat.

tmdb_overview: Bret loses Jemaine's trust after Bret abandons him when they get mugged. To regain it, Bret tries to retrieve Jemaine's stolen "camera phone" (a phone with a camera taped to it) from their muggers, who are experiencing their own trust issues.
